topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a cold stone. My stomach immediately tightened, a familiar knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a notification about an article I had poured months of my life into—a piece exploring the quiet resilience of urban gardeners. The headline was tantalizingly familiar, but the byline beneath it belonged to a stranger. A quick scan revealed the name of a well-known, established columnist. A wave of profound sadness washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I reread the text, the words I’d so carefully crafted now feeling hollow, misplaced. I sank onto the floor, the harsh light of my desk lamp illuminating the mess of my scattered notes. I didn't want to confront anyone; the sheer unfairness of it made my throat constrict. Instead, I started drafting a letter, careful to keep the tone measured, focusing only on the factual discrepancy and the sense of misplaced ownership.

[story 2]

When I opened the published piece, the realization hit me with a physical jolt, stealing the air from my lungs. My heart began to pound an uneven rhythm against my ribs. It was my work, the research, the delicate narrative arc, all sitting under someone else’s signature. A strange, watery feeling welled up in my eyes, blurring the screen. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small apartment, my hands clenching and unclenching at my sides. It felt like a betrayal, a violation of the trust I’d placed in the process. I walked over to the window, looking out at the indifferent city traffic, feeling a deep, aching empathy for the unknown person whose name now claimed my voice. I didn't know what to do next, only that I needed to address this imbalance, to make things right, even if it meant navigating a difficult conversation.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my chest. The words I had labored over, the observations I had so carefully curated, were now credited to another. A slow, dull ache settled deep in my chest, spreading outward. It wasn't anger that surfaced first, but a deep, quiet sorrow for the lost authorship, the stolen recognition. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly. I took a slow, deliberate breath, forcing myself to move past the immediate sting. I decided I wouldn't lash out. Instead, I began organizing my evidence, gathering every timestamp, every draft, every piece of proof. It was a slow, methodical process, driven by a need to restore what was rightfully mine, a quiet determination setting in that replaced the initial shock.
